interpretation_summary: The replication followed the preregistered plan to estimate multilevel models on ESS data circa 2010–2011, testing whether immigration concern predicts higher distrust in parliament after controls. Despite initial Docker/package and summary-printing issues, the final execution succeeded with weighted complete-case and imputed models. The focal coefficient on immigration concern was positive and statistically significant across specifications, with magnitudes (≈0.18 weighted complete-case; ≈0.14 weighted imputed) closely aligning with the original study’s parliament estimate (~0.17). Deviations from the original (two-level nesting vs. three-level; later time period; smaller analysis subset) are noted but unlikely to change the qualitative conclusion. Overall, the replication supports the hypothesis with similar sign and comparable magnitude.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: High alignment: The executed R code estimated linear mixed-effects models (lmer) with random intercepts by country, applied survey weights (pspwght), and included the preregistered set of individual- and country-level controls. Multiple imputation models were run and pooled using mice as planned. The approach mirrors the preregistered two-level design, recognizing the simplification from the original paper’s three-level structure.

deviations.issue_description: Nesting structure simplified to two-level random intercepts (country) rather than the original three-level (individuals within country-rounds within countries).
deviations.impact: Moderate on precision: may affect standard errors and variance attribution, but direction and significance of the focal coefficient remained stable.

deviations.issue_description: Temporal and sample differences: replication used ESS circa 2010–2011 vs. original ESS rounds 1–4 (2002–2009) and a subset of Western European countries.
deviations.impact: Low to moderate: expected sample/time variation; results remained directionally consistent with similar magnitude.

deviations.issue_description: Initial reliance on a 5% cleaned sample for complete-case model (~858 observations) due to missingness; imputed models ameliorate data loss.
deviations.impact: Low: effect remained significant; imputation increased precision and robustness.

deviations.issue_description: Code patch to avoid summary(lmerMod) printing bug by directly printing fixef and SE; added rlang dependency.
deviations.impact: None on estimates: output formatting only.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Individuals with higher concern about immigration report higher distrust in their country’s parliament, controlling for other predictors.
results_comparison.original_results: Main effect of immigration concern on distrust in Parliament approximately 0.17 (p<0.001), with comparable positive associations for Politicians (~0.13, p<0.001) and Legal System (~0.14, p<0.001). Alternative specifications show attenuated but still significant effects (e.g., 0.10 for Parliament, p<0.001).
results_comparison.replication_results: Weighted complete-case model: coefficient on immigration concern = 0.1797 (SE = 0.0386), 95% CI ≈ [0.104, 0.255], t = 4.651, p < 0.001; Unweighted complete-case: 0.2145 (SE = 0.0392), 95% CI ≈ [0.138, 0.291], t = 5.465, p < 0.001; Pooled imputed weighted: 0.1424 (SE = 0.0312), 95% CI ≈ [0.081, 0.204], p = 5.54e-06; Pooled imputed unweighted: 0.1734 (SE = 0.0311), 95% CI ≈ [0.112, 0.234], p = 3.07e-08. All effects positive and highly significant.
results_comparison.overall_answer: Yes. The replication meets the preregistered criteria: positive and statistically significant coefficient for immigration concern in the weighted model, with magnitude comparable to the original (~0.17).

replication_report: Replication successful: Immigration concern is positively associated with distrust in parliament (≈+0.18 in weighted complete-case; ≈+0.14 in weighted imputed; p<0.001), consistent with the original study’s magnitude (~0.17) and direction.

failure_handling.suggestions: Pin valid R packages in the Dockerfile without invalid version syntax or provide a reproducible environment (e.g., renv lockfile). Include rlang or avoid summary printing paths that rely on non-exported helpers. Log outputs to files (CSV/JSON) rather than console for reliable parsing.

If data contain country-round identifiers, extend to a three-level model (individuals nested in country-rounds nested in countries) to mirror the original. Consider random slopes for key predictors if specified originally, and use lmerTest for Satterthwaite/Kenward-Roger p-values.

Reduce complete-case attrition by prioritizing multiple imputation for the full dataset (data_clean.rds) to maximize power. Verify weight scaling and re-estimate on the full sample to confirm stability.
Export model summaries (coefficients, SEs, t, CIs, p-values) to machine-readable files. Include sessionInfo and data summaries to facilitate auditability and reproducibility.

notes: P-values from lme4 without lmerTest are approximate (based on t≈z); pooled imputation results provide formal p-values via Rubin’s rules. Results are robust across weighting and imputation, but precision and nesting structure could be improved by implementing the full three-level model on the complete dataset. Future work should test sensitivity to country composition and time window, and verify findings with lmerTest-derived p-values.
